claim: GetgoYokeDir accepted XDG runtime/cache directories after mkdir alone, so an existing but unwritable runtime directory caused guard, counter, and other state writes to fail at runtime.
evidence: Before the fix, GetgoYokeDir returned immediately after os.MkdirAll on XDG paths and never verified that files could actually be created there; downstream code uses that path for guard locks and tool counters.
runtimeTrigger: Run the app with XDG_RUNTIME_DIR pointing to an existing but read-only directory; skill-guard and related state writers then fail or silently drop to fallback behavior.

claim: Session JSONL readers used the default bufio.Scanner token limit, so large pending-learning and sharp-edge records failed during resume, query, and artifact analysis flows.
evidence: Context and artifact loaders in pkg/session consumed one JSONL record per scanner token without raising the default 64 KiB cap; the same pattern appeared in CheckPendingLearnings, LoadPendingLearnings, QuerySharpEdges, and related readers until a shared scanner helper was added.
runtimeTrigger: Write a valid session artifact line above 64 KiB, then invoke resume/query flows such as CheckPendingLearnings, LoadPendingLearnings, or QuerySharpEdges.

claim: Routing transcript readers used the default bufio.Scanner token limit, so any single JSONL event above 64 KiB caused enforcement, metadata extraction, and task-analysis paths to fail on valid transcripts.
evidence: ParseTranscript, TranscriptAnalyzer.Analyze, and ParseTranscriptForMetadata all consumed one transcript event per scanner token without raising the default 64 KiB cap, while transcript lines can include raw tool payloads and long assistant content.
runtimeTrigger: Record a transcript containing a single large tool event, then run transcript-based enforcement or endstate metadata extraction such as CountChildAgentsFromTranscript, TranscriptAnalyzer.Analyze, or ParseTranscriptForMetadata.

claim: runWaves only returned an error when every member in a wave failed, so final-wave partial failures were reported as successful team runs even though failed members remained.
evidence: Before the fix, partial failures only emitted a warning and execution continued; if the partial failure happened on the last wave there was no later check, so runWaves returned nil and downstream callers treated the run as clean.
runtimeTrigger: Run a team where one member in the last wave fails while another succeeds; the command exits without surfacing the failed member state.

claim: Telemetry JSONL readers used the default bufio.Scanner token limit, so large valid records were dropped or caused load failures across invocation, escalation, scout, collaboration, and agent lifecycle analytics.
evidence: LoadInvocations, LoadEscalations, LoadScoutRecommendations, ReadCollaborationLogs, and ReadAgentLifecycleLogs all scanned one JSONL record per token without increasing the default 64 KiB cap.
runtimeTrigger: Store a telemetry record with a task description, reason, or other field above 64 KiB and then load it through the corresponding telemetry query path.

claim: ReadEndstateLogs used the default bufio.Scanner token limit, so large valid endstate JSONL entries were skipped or surfaced as read errors during workflow analytics.
evidence: Endstate logs serialize full recommendation arrays into one JSONL line, but ReadEndstateLogs previously used the default scanner without raising its token cap.
runtimeTrigger: Write an endstate log entry whose serialized recommendations exceed 64 KiB and then call ReadEndstateLogs or GetAgentStats.

claim: Failure tracker scanning used the default bufio.Scanner token limit, so large valid failure entries stopped loop-detection and clearing logic from seeing matching records.
evidence: scanFailures powers GetFailureCount and ClearFailures but previously scanned one JSONL failure record per default scanner token; large error_match payloads therefore broke matching on otherwise valid records.
runtimeTrigger: Persist a failure-tracker entry with a long error_match field above 64 KiB, then call GetFailureCount or ClearFailures for that file/error combination.

claim: GetgoYokeDataDir accepted XDG data directories after mkdir alone, so existing but unwritable data directories caused telemetry and ML log writes to fail later at runtime.
evidence: GetgoYokeDataDir returned XDG_DATA_HOME or ~/.local/share/goyoke after os.MkdirAll without verifying file creation, while telemetry writers later open files under that path for append.
runtimeTrigger: Run with XDG_DATA_HOME or ~/.local/share/goyoke present but unwritable, then attempt telemetry or ML logging such as agent lifecycle or tool-event writes.

claim: Permission-gate cache filenames embedded raw session_id values, allowing path separator and traversal characters to escape the intended runtime directory.
evidence: cachePath(sessionID) previously formatted sessionID directly into the filename under XDG_RUNTIME_DIR or /tmp. SessionID is taken from stdin tool events, so slash-containing values changed the resolved path.
runtimeTrigger: Feed the permission gate a PreToolUse payload whose session_id contains path separators such as ../../escape/me, then write an allow_session cache entry.

claim: The weekly aggregation tool counted JSONL records with the default scanner limit, so a single large archived record caused line counts to fail and summary stats to be zeroed or degraded.
evidence: aggregateFiles calls countLines before archiving each memory file. countLines previously used bufio.NewScanner with the default 64 KiB token cap, while archived JSONL rows can legitimately exceed that size.
runtimeTrigger: Run goyoke-aggregate on a memory JSONL file containing a single large valid line above 64 KiB.

claim: The TUI telemetry panel still used the default scanner limit when reading routing-decisions JSONL, so large valid decisions could disappear from the UI or raise load errors even after backend readers were fixed.
evidence: loadJSONL scanned each routing decision line with bufio.NewScanner and no enlarged buffer. The panel reads the same decision log that can contain large recommendation payloads.
runtimeTrigger: Open the routing telemetry panel against a routing-decisions file containing a single valid entry above 64 KiB.

claim: IPC bridge startup built Unix socket paths directly from XDG runtime/temp directories without checking sockaddr_un length, so long base paths failed with bind: invalid argument before the TUI accepted MCP connections.
evidence: buildSocketPath previously concatenated the base directory with goyoke-{pid}.sock unconditionally. Linux pathname Unix sockets cap the path length, and the failure reproduced once tests used long temp directory names.
runtimeTrigger: Start the TUI with a long XDG_RUNTIME_DIR or TMPDIR path, or under long generated temp directory names; bridge creation fails and GOYOKE_SOCKET is never usable.
